disease.name: Pediatric Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis)

ageLogic.description: Highest risk 6-36 months, decreasing at 3-5 years, rare >6 years

etiology.note: Most common cause, accounts for majority of cases

westleyCroupScore.description: Westley Croup Score: total 0-17. Classifies severity and guides management.

immediateDangerSigns.description: Inspiratory stridor present when child is calm
Fatigue from increased work of breathing — ominous sign
Minimal air entry despite marked effort — impending respiratory arrest

severeCriteria.description: Severe croup: stridor at rest, significant indrawing, possible hypoxia. Requires urgent intervention.

moderateCriteria.description: Moderate croup: stridor at rest, chest indrawing, no hypoxia, no altered consciousness.

mildCriteria.description: Mild croup: barking cough ± stridor only when agitated, no indrawing at rest, normal SpO2.

historyFeatures.description: Classic seal-like barking cough is pathognomonic
Inspiratory stridor, hallmark of upper airway obstruction
Coryza and mild cough for 1-3 days before stridor

examFeatures.description: Inspiratory stridor = upper airway obstruction. Worse with agitation.

examFeatures.note: Keep child calm; crying exacerbates obstruction

bedside.name: Clinical airway assessment
bedside.indication: assess work of breathing, air entry, consciousness

imaging.name: Anterior-posterior neck X-ray (steeple sign)
imaging.indication: atypical presentation or diagnostic uncertainty only
imaging.findings: Steeple sign (symmetrical subglottic narrowing) confirms croup
imaging.note: Do NOT delay treatment for imaging. Diagnosis is primarily clinical.

laboratory.name: FBC + CRP
laboratory.priority: only if epiglottitis or bacterial tracheitis suspected
laboratory.indication: toxic appearance, high fever, poor response to standard therapy

investigations.noteClinical: DIAGNOSIS IS CLINICAL. Do not examine throat routinely — may precipitate complete airway obstruction. If epiglottitis is suspected, do not examine without airway team ready.

management.protocolReference: Kenya Paediatric Guidelines + Westley-based croup management principles
management.airwayEmergencyNote: If impending respiratory failure (Westley ≥12, altered consciousness, silent chest): immediate anaesthesia/ENT for intubation. Do not wait for imaging.

pathways.criteria: Barking cough only, no stridor at rest, no indrawing, normal SpO2
treatment: Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg PO single dose (max 16 mg)
No stridor at rest — may discharge if caregiver reliable
Counsel on: keeping child calm, paracetamol for fever, return if stridor at rest develops or breathing worsens
Return immediately if: stridor at rest, increased work of breathing, drooling, toxic appearance

pathways.criteria: Stridor at rest, chest indrawing, no hypoxia, no altered consciousness
treatment: Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg PO/IV stat (max 16 mg)
Nebulised Adrenaline (1:1000) 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL) if stridor at rest
Observe for minimum 4 hours after adrenaline administration
Oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥92% if hypoxic
Keep child calm — minimize handling, allow upright position with caregiver
Admit if stridor persists after 4 hours or repeated adrenaline required

pathways.criteria: Stridor at rest + restlessness/agitation, severe indrawing, hypoxia (SpO2 <92%)
treatment: Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg IV stat (max 16 mg)
Nebulised Adrenaline (1:1000) 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL) — may repeat every 1-2 hours
Oxygen to maintain SpO2 >92%
Heliox (70:30 helium:oxygen) if available — reduces work of breathing
Prepare for intubation early — call anaesthesia and ENT
Admit to ICU/HDU
Minimal handling, upright positioning

pathways.criteria: Altered consciousness, lethargy, cyanosis, silent chest, severe hypoxia despite oxygen
treatment: EMERGENCY: Call anaesthesia and ENT for urgent intubation
Intubation with endotracheal tube 0.5-1 size smaller than age-appropriate due to subglottic edema
Nebulised Adrenaline while preparing airway
Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg IV (max 16 mg)
Ventilation in ICU
Consider heliox post-intubation if difficult to ventilate

management.adrenalineDilution: Use standard 1:1000 (1 mg/mL) adrenaline. Dose: 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL). Nebulise over 10-15 minutes with 100% oxygen. Do NOT dilute further.
management.steroidDosing: Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg PO/IV (max 16 mg) single dose. Equivalent to prednisolone 2 mg/kg. One dose is sufficient; repeat only if symptoms recur after initial improvement.

treatmentResponseLogic.expectedResponse: Steroids improve symptoms in 2-4 hours; adrenaline provides temporary relief within 10-15 minutes (duration 1-2 hours).
warningSignsFailure: Repeated adrenaline needed more frequently than every 2 hours
Hypoxia not correcting with oxygen and adrenaline
treatmentResponseLogic.ifFailure: Escalate and consider alternative diagnoses: bacterial tracheitis, epiglottitis, foreign body, retropharyngeal abscess, or complete airway obstruction.

complications.note: Risk if prolonged intubation or traumatic intubation through edematous airway

disease.emergencyPriority: Croup is a REVERSIBLE upper airway obstruction. Steroids are definitive treatment. Nebulised adrenaline is a temporising bridge. Prepare for intubation if severe and not responding.
diagnosticClues: Westley Croup Score guides severity and management
